subject: Mathematics
question_text: Check the continuity of the function f given by f(x) = 2x + 3 at x = 1.
question_html: Check the continuity of the function f given by f(x) = 2x + 3 at x = 1.
solution_text: First of all note that the function is defined at the given point x = 1 and f(1) = 5. Clearly {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} {/tex} (2x + 3) = 2(1) + 3 = 5 Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} f(x){/tex} = 5 = f (1) Hence, f is continuous at x = 1.
solution_html:
First of all note that the function is defined at the given point x = 1 and f(1) = 5.
Clearly
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} {/tex} (2x + 3) = 2(1) + 3 = 5
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} f(x){/tex} = 5 = f (1)
Hence, f is continuous at x = 1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Examine whether the function f given by f(x) = x 2 is continuous at x = 0.
question_html: Examine whether the function f given by f(x) = x2 is continuous at x = 0.
solution_text: First, note that the function is defined at the given point x = 0 and f(0) = 0. Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} x 2 = 0 2 = 0 Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x){/tex} = 0 = f (0) Hence, f is continuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
First, note that the function is defined at the given point x = 0 and f(0) = 0. 
Now,
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} x2 = 02 = 0
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x){/tex} = 0 = f (0)
Hence, f is continuous at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f given by f(x) = | x | at x = 0.
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f given by f(x) = | x | at x = 0.
solution_text: By definition f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {~~~x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Clearly the function is defined at 0 and f(0) = 0. Left-hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^-} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^-} {/tex} (-x) = 0 Similarly, the right-hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^+} {/tex} (x) = 0 Thus, the left-hand limit, right-hand limit and the value of the function coincide at x = 0. Hence, f is continuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
By definition
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {~~~x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Clearly the function is defined at 0 and f(0) = 0.
Left-hand limit of f at 0 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^-} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^-} {/tex} (-x) = 0
Similarly, the right-hand limit of f at 0 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0^+} {/tex} (x) = 0
Thus, the left-hand limit, right-hand limit and the value of the function coincide at x = 0. Hence, f is continuous at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function f given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x^{3}+3,} & {\text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {1,} & {\text { if } x=0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is not continuous at x = 0.
question_html: Show that the function f given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x^{3}+3,} & {\text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {1,} & {\text { if } x=0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is not continuous at x = 0.
solution_text: The function is defined at x = 0 and f(0) =1. When x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, the function is given by a polynomial. Hence, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} (x 3 + 3) = 0 3 + 3 = 3 Since , the limit of f at x = 0 does not coincide with f(0), the function is not continuous at x = 0. It may be noted that x = 0 is the only point of discontinuity for this function.
solution_html:
The function is defined at x = 0 and f(0) =1.
When x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, the function is given by a polynomial. Hence,
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} (x3 + 3) = 03 + 3 = 3
Since , the limit of f at x = 0 does not coincide with f(0), the function is not continuous at x = 0. It may be noted that
x = 0 is the only point of discontinuity for this function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Check the points where the constant function f(x) = k is continuous.
question_html: Check the points where the constant function f(x) = k is continuous.
solution_text: The function is defined at all real numbers and by definition, its value at any real number equals k. Let c be any real number. Then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} k = k Since f(c) = k = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} for any real number c, the function f is continuous at every real number.
solution_html:
The function is defined at all real numbers and by definition, its value at any real number equals k.
Let c be any real number. Then
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} k = k
Since f(c) = k = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} for any real number c, the function f is continuous at every real number.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the identity function on real numbers given by f(x) = x is continuous at every real number.
question_html: Prove that the identity function on real numbers given by f(x) = x is continuous at every real number.
solution_text: The function is clearly defined at every point and f(c) = c for every real number c. Also, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} x = c Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = c = f(c) and hence the function is continuous at every real number c {tex}\in {/tex} R.
solution_html:
The function is clearly defined at every point and f(c) = c for every real number c. Also,
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} x = c
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = c = f(c)
and hence the function is continuous at every real number c {tex}\in {/tex} R.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Is the function defined by f(x) = | x |, a continuous function?
question_html: Is the function defined by f(x) = | x |, a continuous function?
solution_text: We can write f as {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {-x,} & {\text { if } x<0} \\ {~~~x,} & {\text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} we know that f is continuous at x = 0. Suppose c be a real number such that c < 0. Then f(c) = – c. Also {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (-x) = -c Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) {/tex} = f(c), f is continuous at all negative real numbers. Now, suppose c be a real number such that c > 0. Then f(c) = c. Also {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} x = c Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f(c), f is continuous at all positive real numbers. Therefore, f is continuous at all points.
solution_html:
We can write f as
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {-x,} & {\text { if } x<0} \\ {~~~x,} & {\text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
we know that f is continuous at x = 0.
Suppose c be a real number such that c < 0. Then f(c) = – c. Also
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (-x) = -c 
Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) {/tex} = f(c), f is continuous at all negative real numbers.
Now, suppose c be a real number such that c > 0. Then f(c) = c. Also
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex}x = c
Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f(c), f is continuous at all positive real numbers. Therefore, f is continuous at all points.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function 'f ' given by f (x) = x 3 + x 2 – 1.
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function 'f ' given by f (x) = x3 + x2 – 1.
solution_text: Clearly, f is defined at every real number c and f(c) = c 3 + c 2 – 1. Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (x 3 + x 2 − 1) = c 3 + c 2 − 1 = f(c) Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f(c), and hence f is continuous at every real number. This means f is a continuous function.
solution_html:
Clearly, f is defined at every real number c and f(c) = c3 + c2 – 1.
Now,
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (x3 + x2 − 1) = c3 + c2 − 1 = f(c)
Thus,  {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f(c), and hence f is continuous at every real number.
This means f is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f defined by f (x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f defined by f (x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0{/tex}
solution_text: Fix any non zero real number c, we have {tex}\,\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{c}{/tex} Also, since for c {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, f (c) = {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex} , we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f (c) and hence, f is continuous at every point in the domain of f. Thus f is a continuous function.
solution_html:
Fix any non zero real number c, we have
{tex}\,\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{c}{/tex}
Also, since for c {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, f (c) = {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex}, we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f (c) and hence, f is continuous at every point in the domain of f. Thus f is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x+2, \text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x-2, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x+2, \text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x-2, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: The function f is defined at all points of the real line. Case 1: If c < 1, then f(c) = c + 2. Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (x + 2) = c + 2 Thus, f is continuous at all real numbers less than 1. Case 2: If c > 1, then f(c) = c - 2. Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (x - 2) = c - 2 = f (c) Thus, f is continuous at all points x > 1. Case 3: If c = 1, then the left-hand limit of f at x = 1 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1^-} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1^-} {/tex} (x + 2) = 1 + 2 = 3 The right hand limit of f at x = 1 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1^+} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c^+} {/tex} (x - 2) = 1 - 2 = -1 Since the left and right hand limits of f at x = 1 do not coincide, f is not continuous at x = 1. Hence x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.
solution_html:
The function f is defined at all points of the real line.

Case 1: If c < 1, then f(c) = c + 2.
Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex}(x + 2) = c + 2
Thus, f is continuous at all real numbers less than 1.
Case 2: If c > 1, then f(c) = c - 2.
Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} (x - 2) = c - 2 = f (c)
Thus, f is continuous at all points x > 1.
Case 3: If c = 1, then the left-hand limit of f at x = 1 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1^-} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1^-} {/tex}(x + 2) = 1 + 2 = 3
The right hand limit of f at x = 1 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1^+} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c^+} {/tex}(x - 2) = 1 - 2 = -1
Since the left and right hand limits of f at x = 1 do not coincide, f is not continuous at x = 1.
Hence x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all the points of discontinuity of the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {x+2,} & {\text { if } x<1} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } x=1} \\ {x-2,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all the points of discontinuity of the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} {x+2,} & {\text { if } x<1} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } x=1} \\ {x-2,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: f is continuous at all real numbers such that x {tex}\neq{/tex} 1. The left-hand limit of f at x = 1 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex} (x + 2) = 1 + 2 = 3 The right hand limit of f at x = 1 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} {/tex} (x - 2) = 1 - 2 = -1 Since the left and right-hand limits of f at x = 1 do not coincide, f is not continuous at x = 1. Hence x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.
solution_html:
f is continuous at all real numbers such that x {tex}\neq{/tex} 1. The left-hand limit of f at x = 1 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex}(x + 2) = 1 + 2 = 3
The right hand limit of f at x = 1 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} {/tex}(x - 2) = 1 - 2 = -1

Since the left and right-hand limits of f at x = 1 do not coincide, f is not continuous at x = 1. Hence x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {x+2, \text { if } x<0} \\ {-x+2, \text { if } x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html:
Discuss the continuity of the function defined by
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {x+2, \text { if } x<0} \\ {-x+2, \text { if } x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: Observe that the function is defined at all real numbers except at 0. The domain of definition of this function is D 1 {tex}\cup{/tex} D 2 where D 1 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0} and D 2 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x > 0} Case 1: If c {tex}\in{/tex} D 1 , then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \,\,{/tex} (x + 2) = c + 2 = f (c) and hence f is continuous in D 1 . Case 2: If c {tex}\in{/tex} D 2 , then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \,\,{/tex} (-x + 2) = – c + 2 = f (c) and hence f is continuous in D 2 . Since f is continuous at all points in the domain of f, we deduce that f is continuous. The graph of this function is given in the figure. Note that to graph this function we need to lift the pen from the plane of the paper, but we need to do that only for those points where the function is not defined.
solution_html:
Observe that the function is defined at all real numbers except at 0. The domain of definition of this function is
D1 {tex}\cup{/tex} D2 where D1 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0} and D2 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x > 0}
Case 1: If c {tex}\in{/tex} D1, then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \,\,{/tex}(x + 2)
= c + 2 = f (c) and hence f is continuous in D1.
Case 2: If c {tex}\in{/tex} D2, then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \,\,{/tex}(-x + 2)
= – c + 2 = f (c)

and hence f is continuous in D2. Since f is continuous at all points in the domain of f, we deduce that f is continuous. The graph of this function is given in the figure. Note that to graph this function we need to lift the pen from the plane of the paper, but we need to do that only for those points where the function is not defined.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x,} & {\text { if } x \geq 0} \\ {x^{2},} & {\text { if } x<0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html:
Discuss the continuity of the function f given by
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x,} & {\text { if } x \geq 0} \\ {x^{2},} & {\text { if } x<0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: Clearly, the function is defined at every real number. The graph of the function is given in figure. By inspection, it seems prudent to partition the domain of definition of f into three disjoint subsets of the real line. Let D 1 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0}, D 2 = {0} and D 3 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x > 0} Case 1: At any point in D 1 , we have f(x) = x 2 and it is easy to see that it is continuous there Case 2: At any point in D 3 , we have f(x) = x and it is easy to see that it is continuous there Case 3: Now we analyse the function at x = 0. The value of the function at 0 is f(0) = 0. The left hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} {x^2} = {0^2} = 0{/tex} The right-hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} x = 0{/tex} Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x){/tex} = 0 = f(0) and hence f is continuous at 0. This means that f is continuous at every point in its domain and hence, f is a continuous function.
solution_html:
Clearly, the function is defined at every real number. The graph of the function is given in figure. By inspection, it seems prudent to partition the domain of definition of f into three disjoint subsets of the real line.

Let D1 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0}, D2 = {0} and D3 = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x > 0}
Case 1: At any point in D1 , we have f(x) = x2 and it is easy to see that it is continuous there
Case 2: At any point in D3 , we have f(x) = x and it is easy to see that it is continuous there
Case 3: Now we analyse the function at x = 0. The value of the function at 0 is f(0) = 0. The left hand limit of f at 0 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} {x^2} = {0^2} = 0{/tex}
The right-hand limit of f at 0 is
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} x = 0{/tex}
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x){/tex} = 0 = f(0) and hence f is continuous at 0. This means that f is continuous at every point in its domain and hence, f is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that every polynomial function is continuous.
question_html: Show that every polynomial function is continuous.
solution_text: Recall that a function p is a polynomial function if it is defined by p(x) = a 0 + a 1 x + ... + a n x n for some natural number n, a n {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 and a i ∈ R. Clearly this function is defined for every real number. For a fixed real number c, we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} p(x){/tex} = p (c) By definition, p is continuous at c. Since c is any real number, p is continuous at every real number and hence p is a continuous function.
solution_html:
Recall that a function p is a polynomial function if it is defined by p(x) = a0 + a1 x + ... + an xn for some natural number n, an {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 and ai ∈ R. Clearly this function is defined for every real number. For a fixed real number c, we have
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} p(x){/tex}= p (c)
By definition, p is continuous at c. Since c is any real number, p is continuous at every real number and hence p is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all the points of discontinuity of the greatest integer function defined by f(x) = [x], where [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x.
question_html: Find all the points of discontinuity of the greatest integer function defined by f(x) = [x], where [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x.
solution_text: First, observe that f is defined for all real numbers. The graph of the function is given in the figure. From the graph, it looks like that f is discontinuous at every integral point. Below we explore if this is true. Case 1: Let c be a real number which is not equal to any integer. It is evident from the graph that for all real numbers close to c the value of the function is equal to [c]; i.e., {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \,[x] = [c]{/tex} . Also f(c) = [c] and hence the function is continuous at all real numbers not equal to integers. Case 2: Let c be an integer. Then we can find a sufficiently small real number r > 0 such that [c – r] = c – 1 whereas [c + r] = c. This, in terms of limits mean that {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {c^ - }} f(x) = c - 1,\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {c^ + }} f(x) = c{/tex} Since these limits cannot be equal to each other for any c, the function is discontinuous at every integral point.
solution_html:
First, observe that f is defined for all real numbers. The graph of the function is given in the figure. From the graph, it looks like that f is discontinuous at every integral point. Below we explore if this is true.

Case 1: Let c be a real number which is not equal to any integer. It is evident from the graph that for all real numbers close to c the value of the function is equal to [c]; i.e., {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} \,[x] = [c]{/tex}. Also f(c) = [c] and hence the function is continuous at all real numbers not equal to integers.
Case 2: Let c be an integer. Then we can find a sufficiently small real number r > 0 such that [c – r] = c – 1 whereas [c + r] = c.
This, in terms of limits mean that
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {c^ - }} f(x) = c - 1,\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {c^ + }} f(x) = c{/tex}
Since these limits cannot be equal to each other for any c, the function is discontinuous at every integral point.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that every rational function is continuous.
question_html: Prove that every rational function is continuous.
solution_text: Recall that every rational function f is given by {tex}f(x)=\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}, q(x) \neq 0{/tex} where p and q are polynomial functions. The domain of f is all real numbers except points at which q is zero. Since polynomial functions are continuous, f is continuous
solution_html:
Recall that every rational function f is given by
{tex}f(x)=\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}, q(x) \neq 0{/tex}
where p and q are polynomial functions. The domain of f is all real numbers except points at which q is zero. Since polynomial functions are continuous, f is continuous

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of sine function.
question_html: Discuss the continuity of sine function.
solution_text: To see this we use the following facts {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} sin x = 0 = sin 0. So, sin x is continuous at x = 0. Now, observe that f(x) = sin x is defined for every real number. Let c be a real number. Put x = c + h. If x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} c then, h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0. Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} sin x = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} sin (c + h) = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} [sin c cos h + cos c sin h] = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} [sin c cos h] + {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} [cos c sin h] = sin c + 0 = sin c = f (c) Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f(c) and hence f is a continuous function.
solution_html:
To see this we use the following facts
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} sin x = 0 = sin 0. So, sin x is continuous at x = 0.
Now, observe that f(x) = sin x is defined for every real number. Let c be a real number. Put x = c + h. If x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} c then, h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0. Therefore,
   {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} {/tex} sin x
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} sin (c + h)
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} [sin c cos h + cos c sin h]
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex} [sin c cos h] + {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} {/tex}[cos c sin h]
= sin c + 0 = sin c = f (c)
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to c} f(x){/tex} = f(c) and hence f is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function defined by f(x) = tan x is a continuous function.
question_html: Prove that the function defined by f(x) = tan x is a continuous function.
solution_text: The function f (x) = tan x = {tex}\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex} . This is defined for all real numbers such that cos x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, i.e., x {tex}\neq{/tex} (2n +1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . We know that both sine and cosine functions are continuous. Thus tan x being a quotient of two continuous functions is continuous wherever it is defined i.e, in its domain of definition.
solution_html: The function f (x) = tan x = {tex}\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}{/tex}. This is defined for all real numbers such that cos x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, i.e., x {tex}\neq{/tex} (2n +1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}. We know that both sine and cosine functions are continuous. Thus tan x being a quotient of two continuous functions is continuous wherever it is defined i.e, in its domain of definition.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function defined by f(x) = sin (x 2 ) is a continuous function.
question_html: Show that the function defined by f(x) = sin (x2) is a continuous function.
solution_text: Observe that the function is defined for every real number. The function f may be thought of as a composition g o h of the two functions g and h, where g (x) = sin x and h (x) = x 2 . Since both g and h are continuous functions, Suppose f and g are real valued functions such that (f o g) is defined at c. If g is continuous at c and if f is continuous at g (c), then (f o g) is continuous at c.
solution_html: Observe that the function is defined for every real number. The function f may be thought of as a composition g o h of the two functions g and h, where g (x) = sin x and h (x) = x2. Since both g and h are continuous functions, Suppose f and g are real valued functions such that (f o g) is defined at c. If g is continuous at c and if f is continuous at g (c), then (f o g) is continuous at c.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function f defined by f(x) = |1 – x + | x | |, where x is any real number, is a continuous function.
question_html: Show that the function f defined by f(x) = |1 – x + | x | |, where x is any real number, is a continuous function.
solution_text: Define g by g (x) = 1 – x + | x| and h by h (x) = | x| for all real x. Then (h o g) (x) = h (g (x)) = h (1– x + | x |) = | 1– x + | x | | = f(x) h is a continuous function. Hence g being a sum of a polynomial function and the modulus function is continuous. But then f being a composition of two continuous functions is continuous.
solution_html:
Define g by g (x) = 1 – x + | x| and h by h (x) = | x| for all real x. Then
(h o g) (x) = h (g (x))
= h (1– x + | x |)
= | 1– x + | x | | = f(x)
h is a continuous function. Hence g being a sum of a polynomial function and the modulus function is continuous. But then f being a composition of two continuous functions is continuous.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the derivative of the function given by f(x) = sin (x 2 ).
question_html: Find the derivative of the function given by f(x) = sin (x2).
solution_text: Observe that the given function is a composite of two functions. Indeed, if t = u(x) = x 2 and v(t) = sin t, then f(x) = (v o u) (x) = v(u(x)) = v(x 2 ) = sin x 2 Put t = u(x) = x 2 . Observe that {tex}\frac{d v}{d t}{/tex} = cost and {tex}\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} = 2x exist. Hence, by chain rule {tex}\frac{d f}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} = cost.2x It is normal practice to express the final result only in terms of x. Thus {tex}\frac{d f}{d x}{/tex} = cost. 2x = 2x cos x 2
solution_html:
Observe that the given function is a composite of two functions. Indeed, if t = u(x) = x2 and v(t) = sin t, then
f(x) = (v o u) (x) = v(u(x)) = v(x2) = sin x2
Put t = u(x) = x2. Observe that {tex}\frac{d v}{d t}{/tex} = cost and {tex}\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} = 2x exist. Hence, by chain rule
{tex}\frac{d f}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} = cost.2x
It is normal practice to express the final result only in terms of x. Thus
{tex}\frac{d f}{d x}{/tex} = cost. 2x = 2x cos x2

question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} if x - y = {tex}\pi{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} if x - y = {tex}\pi{/tex}
solution_text: The given equation is: x {tex}-{/tex} y = {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x – {tex}\pi{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1

 x {tex}-{/tex} y = {tex}\pi{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if y + sin y = cos x.
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if y + sin y = cos x.
solution_text: We differentiate the relationship directly with respect to x, i.e., {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sin y) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (cos x) which implies using the chain rule {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\cos y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - sin x This gives {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos y}{/tex} where {tex}y \neq(2 n+1) \pi{/tex}
solution_html:
We differentiate the relationship directly with respect to x, i.e.,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}(sin y) = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (cos x)
which implies using the chain rule
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\cos y \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= - sin x
This gives {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos y}{/tex}
where {tex}y \neq(2 n+1) \pi{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the derivative of f given by f(x) = sin –1 x assuming it exists.
question_html: Find the derivative of f given by f(x) = sin–1 x assuming it exists.
solution_html:
Let y = sin–1 x. Then, x = sin y
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
1 = cos y {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
which implies that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos y}=\frac{1}{\cos \left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)}{/tex}
Observe that this is defined only for cos y {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, i.e., sin–1 x {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, i.e., x {tex}\neq{/tex} - 1, 1, i.e., x {tex}\in{/tex} (– 1, 1).
To make this result a bit more attractive, we carry out the following manipulation. Recall , that for x ∈ (– 1, 1), sin (sin–1 x) = x and hence
cos2 y = 1 – (sin y)2 = 1 – (sin (sin–1 x))2 = 1 – x2
{tex}\implies{/tex} cos y = {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}{/tex}
Also, since y {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}, cos y is positive and hence cos y = {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex}
Thus, for x ∈ (– 1, 1) 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos y}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Is it true that x = e log x for all real x?
question_html: Is it true that x = elog x for all real x?
solution_text: First, observe that the domain of log function is a set of all positive real numbers. So the above equation is not true for non-positive real numbers. Now, let y = e log x . If y > 0, we may take logarithm which gives us log y = log (e log x ) = log x . log e = log x. Thus y = x. Hence x = e log x is true only for positive values of x.
solution_html:
First, observe that the domain of log function is a set of all positive real numbers. So the above equation is not true for non-positive real numbers.
Now, let y = elog x.
If y > 0, we may take logarithm which gives us
log y = log (elog x) = log x . log e = log x.
Thus y = x.
Hence x = elog x is true only for positive values of x.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
solution_text: Let y = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{\left(3 x^{2}+4 x+5\right)}}{/tex} Taking logarithm on both sides, we have log y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [log (x – 3) + log (x 2 + 4) – log (3x 2 + 4x + 5)] Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}~\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{\left(3 x^{2}+4 x+5\right)}}{/tex}
Taking logarithm on both sides, we have
log y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[log (x – 3) + log (x2 + 4) – log (3x2 + 4x + 5)]
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}~\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate a x w.r.t. x, where a is a positive constant.
question_html: Differentiate ax w.r.t. x, where a is a positive constant.
solution_text: Let y = a x . Then log y = x log a Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = log a or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y log a Thus {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (a x ) = a x log a
solution_html:
Let y = ax . Then
log y = x log a
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we have
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = log a
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y log a
Thus {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex}(ax) = ax log a

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate x sin x , x > 0 w.r.t. x.
question_html: Differentiate xsin x, x > 0 w.r.t. x.
solution_text: Let y = x sin x . Taking logarithm on both sides, we have log y = {tex}sinx\times logx{/tex} Therefore {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)+\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x){/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x) \frac{1}{x}{/tex} + log x cos x or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \log x\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \log x\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex} x ^{sinx-1}{/tex} sin x + x sin x . cos x log x
solution_html:
Let y = xsin x.
Taking logarithm on both sides, we have
log y = {tex}sinx\times logx{/tex} 
Therefore {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)+\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x){/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x) \frac{1}{x}{/tex} + log x cos x
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=y\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \log x\right]{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \log x\right]{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}  ={tex} x ^{sinx-1}{/tex} sin x + xsin x . cos x log x

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} if y x + x y + x x = a b
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} if yx + xy + xx = ab
solution_text: Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex} , then {tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ...(1) {tex}u = {y^x}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex} {tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left( {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right){/tex} ...(2) {tex}v = {x^y}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex} {tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(3) {tex}w = {x^x}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex} {tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex} ... (4) {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x}}{/tex} (by putting(2), (3) and (4) in (1))
solution_html:
Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex}, then

{tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ...(1)

{tex}u = {y^x}{/tex}

Taking log both sides, we get

{tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex}

{tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex}

Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x

{tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left( {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right){/tex}...(2)

{tex}v = {x^y}{/tex}

Taking log both sides, we get

{tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex}

{tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex}...(3)

{tex}w = {x^x}{/tex}

Taking log both sides, we get

{tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex}

{tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex}... (4)

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x}}{/tex} (by putting(2), (3) and (4) in (1))

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} .
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}.
solution_text: Given that x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Therefore {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-a \sin \theta, \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \cos \theta{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a \cos \theta}{-a \sin \theta}=-\cot \theta{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that
x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
Therefore {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-a \sin \theta, \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \cos \theta{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a \cos \theta}{-a \sin \theta}=-\cot \theta{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if x = at 2 , y = 2at.
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if x = at2 , y = 2at.
solution_text: Given that x = at 2 , y = 2at So {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 2at and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = 2a Therefore {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{2 a}{2 a t}=\frac{1}{t}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that x = at2 , y = 2at
So {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = 2at and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = 2a
Therefore {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{2 a}{2 a t}=\frac{1}{t}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if x = a ( {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = a (1 – cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ).
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if x = a ({tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex}), y = a (1 – cos {tex}\theta{/tex}).
solution_text: Here, {tex}x=a(\theta+sin\theta),~~y=a(1-cos\theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = a(1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a \sin \theta}{a(1+\cos \theta)}=\tan \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}x=a(\theta+sin\theta),~~y=a(1-cos\theta){/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex}   {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = a(1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex}), and  {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
Hence,  {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a \sin \theta}{a(1+\cos \theta)}=\tan \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if {tex}x^{\frac{2}{3}}+y^{\frac{2}{3}}=a^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} .
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if {tex}x^{\frac{2}{3}}+y^{\frac{2}{3}}=a^{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}.
solution_text: Let x = a cos 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = aSin 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} Then {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = -3a cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = 3a sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}{-3 a \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta}=-\tan \theta=-\sqrt[3]{\frac{y}{x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let x = a cos3 {tex}\theta{/tex},  y = aSin3{tex}\theta{/tex}
Then {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = -3a cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
  and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = 3a sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
Therefore,  {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}{-3 a \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta}=-\tan \theta=-\sqrt[3]{\frac{y}{x}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} , if y = x 3 + tan x.
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}, if y = x3 + tan x.
solution_text: Given that y = x 3 + tan x. Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} =3x 2 + sec 2 x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(3 x^{2}+\sec ^{2} x\right){/tex} = 6x + 2 sec x. sec x tan x = 6x + 2 sec 2 x tan x
solution_html:
Given that y = x3 + tan x. Then
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}=3x2 + sec2 x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(3 x^{2}+\sec ^{2} x\right){/tex}
= 6x + 2 sec x. sec x tan x
= 6x + 2 sec2 x tan x

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If y = A sin x + B cos x, then prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0
question_html: If y = A sin x + B cos x, then prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0
solution_text: We have {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = A cos x - B sin x and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (A cos x – B sin x) = – A sin x – B cos x = – y Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = A cos x - B sin x
and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (A cos x – B sin x)
= – A sin x – B cos x = – y
Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + y = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If y = 3e 2x + 2e 3x , prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 0.
question_html: If y = 3e2x + 2e3x , prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 0.
solution_text: Given that y = 3e 2x + 2e 3x , Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 6e 2x + 6e 3x = 6 (e 2x + e 3x ) Therefore, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 12e 2x + 18e 3x = 6 (2e 2x + 3e 3x ) Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 6 (2e 2x + 3e 3x ) – 30 (e 2x + e 3x ) + 6 (3e 2x + 2e 3x ) = 0. So, the result is correct.
solution_html:
Given that y = 3e2x + 2e3x,
Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 6e2x + 6e3x
= 6 (e2x + e3x)
Therefore, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 12e2x + 18e3x
= 6 (2e2x + 3e3x)
Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 6 (2e2x + 3e3x) – 30 (e2x + e3x) + 6 (3e2x + 2e3x) = 0.
So, the result is correct.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: If y = sin –1 x, show that (1 – x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} .
question_html: If y = sin–1 x, show that (1 – x2) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}.
solution_text: We have y = sin –1 x. Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)})=0{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex} Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
We have y = sin–1x. Then 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex}
or  {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex}
So,  {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0{/tex}
or  {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)})=0{/tex}
or  {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex}
Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x, the function, {tex}\sqrt{3 x+2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x^{2}+4}}{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x, the function,{tex}\sqrt{3 x+2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x^{2}+4}}{/tex}
solution_text: Let y = {tex}\sqrt{3 x+2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x^{2}+4}}{/tex} or y = {tex}(3x+2)^\frac12+(2x^2+4)^{-\frac12}{/tex} Note that this function is defined at all real numbers x > - {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} . Therefore {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}(3 x+2)^{\frac{1}{2}-1}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(3 x+2)+\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(2 x^{2}+4\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+4\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(3 x+2)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot(3)-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(2 x^{2}+4\right)^{-\frac{3}{2}} \cdot 4 x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2 \sqrt{3 x+2}}-\frac{2 x}{\left(2 x^{2}+4\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} This is defined for all real numbers x > - {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let  y = {tex}\sqrt{3 x+2}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x^{2}+4}}{/tex}
 or   y = {tex}(3x+2)^\frac12+(2x^2+4)^{-\frac12}{/tex}
Note that this function is defined at all real numbers x > -{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}. Therefore
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}(3 x+2)^{\frac{1}{2}-1}{/tex}.{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(3 x+2)+\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(2 x^{2}+4\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}-1} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+4\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}(3 x+2)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \cdot(3)-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(2 x^{2}+4\right)^{-\frac{3}{2}} \cdot 4 x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{2 \sqrt{3 x+2}}-\frac{2 x}{\left(2 x^{2}+4\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex}
This is defined for all real numbers x > -{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x, the function, log 7 (log x)
question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x, the function, log7 (log x)
solution_text: Let y = log 7 (log x) = {tex}\frac{\log (\log x)}{\log 7}{/tex} ...(by change of base formula) The function is defined for all real numbers x > 1. Therefore {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log 7} \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (log (log x)) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\log 7} \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log 7 \log x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let  y = log7 (log x) = {tex}\frac{\log (\log x)}{\log 7}{/tex}    ...(by change of base formula)
The function is defined for all real numbers x > 1. Therefore
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log 7} \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (log (log x))
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\log 7} \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x \log 7 \log x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the cos –1 (sin x) w.r.t. x.
question_html: Differentiate the cos–1 (sin x) w.r.t. x.
solution_text: Let f (x) = cos –1 (sin x). Observe that this function is defined for all real numbers. We may rewrite this function as f(x) = cos –1 (sin x)} = cos -1 {tex}\left[\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-x{/tex} Thus f ′(x) = –1.
solution_html:
Let f (x) = cos–1 (sin x). Observe that this function is defined for all real numbers. We may rewrite this function as
f(x) = cos–1 (sin x)}
= cos-1{tex}\left[\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-x{/tex}
Thus f ′(x) = –1.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right){/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_html: Differentiate the {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right){/tex} w.r.t. x.
solution_text: Let f(x) = tan –1 {tex}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right){/tex} . Observe that this function is defined for all real numbers, where cos x {tex}\neq{/tex} – 1; i.e., at all odd multiples of {tex}\pi{/tex} . We may rewrite this function as f(x) = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right){/tex} = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex} f(x) = tan -1 {tex}\left[\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f^\prime(x)= \frac12{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = tan–1{tex}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right){/tex}. Observe that this function is defined for all real numbers, where cos x {tex}\neq{/tex} – 1; i.e., at all odd multiples of {tex}\pi{/tex}. We may rewrite this function as
f(x) = tan-1{tex}\left(\frac{\sin x}{1+\cos x}\right){/tex}
= tan-1{tex}\left[\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{x}{2}}\right]{/tex}
f(x) = tan-1{tex}\left[\tan \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}f^\prime(x)= \frac12{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the sin –1 {tex}\left(\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}}\right){/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_html: Differentiate the sin–1{tex}\left(\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}}\right){/tex} w.r.t. x.
solution_text: Let f(x) = sin –1 {tex}\left(\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}}\right){/tex} . To find the domain of this function we need to find all x such that {tex}-1 \leq \frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}} \leq 1{/tex} . Since the quantity in the middle is always positive, We need to find all x such that {tex}\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}} \leq{/tex} 1, i.e., all x such that 2 x + 1 {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 + 4x . We may rewrite this as 2 {tex}\leq \frac{1}{2^{x}}{/tex} + {tex}2^x{/tex} which is true for all x. Hence the function is defined at every real number. By putting 2x = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} , this function may be rewritten as {tex}f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{2^{x} \cdot 2}{1+\left(2^{x}\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right]{/tex} = sin –1 [sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ] = 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 tan –1 (2 x ) Thus {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 \cdot \frac{1}{1+\left(2^{x}\right)^{2}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(2^{x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{1+4^{x}} \cdot\left(2^{x}\right) \log 2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2^{x+1} \log 2}{1+4^{x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = sin–1{tex}\left(\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}}\right){/tex}. To find the domain of this function we need to find all x such that {tex}-1 \leq \frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}} \leq 1{/tex}. Since the quantity in the middle is always positive,
We need to find all x such that {tex}\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}} \leq{/tex} 1, i.e., all x such that 2x + 1 {tex}\leq{/tex} 1 + 4x . We may rewrite this as 2 {tex}\leq \frac{1}{2^{x}}{/tex} + {tex}2^x{/tex} which is true for all x. Hence the function is defined at every real number. By putting 2x = tan {tex}\theta{/tex}, this function may be rewritten as
{tex}f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{2^{x+1}}{1+4^{x}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{2^{x} \cdot 2}{1+\left(2^{x}\right)^{2}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 \tan \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right]{/tex}
= sin–1 [sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex}]
= 2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 tan–1 (2x)
Thus {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=2 \cdot \frac{1}{1+\left(2^{x}\right)^{2}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(2^{x}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2}{1+4^{x}} \cdot\left(2^{x}\right) \log 2{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2^{x+1} \log 2}{1+4^{x}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find f ′(x) if f(x) = (sin x) sin x for all 0 < x < {tex}\pi{/tex} .
question_html: Find f ′(x) if f(x) = (sin x)sin x for all 0 < x < {tex}\pi{/tex}.
solution_text: Clearly, the function, y = (sin x) sin x is defined for all positive real numbers. Taking logarithms, we have log y = log (sin x) sin x = sin x log (sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sin x log (sin x)) = cos x log (sin x) + sin x. {tex}\frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sin x) = cos x log (sin x) + cos x = (1 + log (sin x)) cos x Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} = y((1 + log (sin x)) cos x) i.e, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log (sin x)) ( sin x) sin x cos x
solution_html:
Clearly, the function, y = (sin x)sin x  is defined for all positive real numbers.
Taking logarithms, we have
log y = log (sin x)sin x = sin x log (sin x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sin x log (sin x))
= cos x log (sin x) + sin x.{tex}\frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (sin x)
= cos x log (sin x) + cos x
= (1 + log (sin x)) cos x
Thus,  {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} = y((1 + log (sin x)) cos x)
i.e,      {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log (sin x)) ( sin x)sin x cos x

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For a positive constant a find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} , wherer {tex}y = {a^{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)}}{/tex} and {tex}x = {\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)^a}{/tex} .
question_html: For a positive constant a find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}, wherer {tex}y = {a^{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)}}{/tex} and {tex}x = {\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)^a}{/tex}.
solution_text: {tex}y = {a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = {a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}.\log a.\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}x = {\left( {t + \frac{1}{{{t}}}} \right)^a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)^{a - 1}}.\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}.\log a\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right)}}{{a{{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)}^{a - 1}}\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}.\log a}}{{a{{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)}^{a - 1}}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y = {a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = {a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}.\log a.\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right){/tex}

{tex}x = {\left( {t + \frac{1}{{{t}}}} \right)^a}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)^{a - 1}}.\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}.\log a\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right)}}{{a{{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)}^{a - 1}}\left( {1 - \frac{1}{{{t^2}}}} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{{a^{t + \frac{1}{t}}}.\log a}}{{a{{\left( {t + \frac{1}{t}} \right)}^{a - 1}}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate sin 2 x w.r.t. e cos x .
question_html: Differentiate sin2 x w.r.t. ecos x.
solution_text: Let u (x) = sin 2 x and v (x) = e cos x . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}{/tex} . Clearly {tex}\frac{d u}{d x} {/tex} = 2 sin x cos x and {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e cos x (– sin x) = – (sin x) e cos x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{-\sin x e^{\cos x}}=-\frac{2 \cos x}{e^{\cos x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let u (x) = sin2 x and v (x) = ecos x . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}{/tex}.
Clearly {tex}\frac{d u}{d x} {/tex} = 2 sin x cos x
and      {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = ecos x (– sin x) = – (sin x) ecos x
{tex}\therefore{/tex}         {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{-\sin x e^{\cos x}}=-\frac{2 \cos x}{e^{\cos x}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Prove that the function f(x) = 5x - 3 is continuous at x = 0, at x = -3 and at x = 5
question_html: Prove that the function f(x) = 5x - 3 is continuous at x = 0, at x = -3 and at x = 5
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x) = 5x - 3, ~~at~~ x = 0{/tex} , Clearly, {tex}f(0) = 5 \times0 - 3 = -3{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} (5x - 3) = 5 \times 0 - 3 = - 3{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = f(0){/tex} Therefore, f is continuous at x = 0 At x = -3, f(-3) = {tex}5 \times(-3)-3=-18{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} (5x - 3) = 5 \times ( - 3) - 3 = - 18{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} f(x) = f( - 3){/tex} Therefore, f is continuous at x = -3 {tex}At~~ x = 5, f(5) = 5\times5-3=22{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} (5x - 3) = 5 \times 5 - 3 = 22{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} f(x) = f(5){/tex} Therefore, f is continuous at = 5
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x) = 5x - 3, ~~at~~ x = 0{/tex},
Clearly, {tex}f(0) = 5 \times0 - 3 = -3{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} (5x - 3) = 5 \times 0 - 3 = - 3{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = f(0){/tex} 
Therefore, f is continuous at x = 0
At x = -3, f(-3) = {tex}5 \times(-3)-3=-18{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} (5x - 3) = 5 \times ( - 3) - 3 = - 18{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} f(x) = f( - 3){/tex} 
Therefore, f is continuous at x = -3
{tex}At~~ x = 5, f(5) =  5\times5-3=22{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} (5x - 3) = 5 \times 5 - 3 = 22{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} f(x) = f(5){/tex} 
Therefore, f is continuous at  = 5

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Examine the continuity of the function f(x) = 2x 2 - 1 at x = 3.
question_html: Examine the continuity of the function f(x) = 2x2 - 1 at x = 3.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = 2{x^2} - 1{/tex} Continuity at x = 3 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \left( {2{x^2} - 1} \right) = 2{\left( 3 \right)^2} - 1 = 18 - 1 = 17{/tex} And {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 2{\left( 3 \right)^2} - 1 = 18 - 1 = 17{/tex} Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f\left( x \right) = f\left( x \right),{/tex} therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 3.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = 2{x^2} - 1{/tex} 

Continuity at x = 3 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \left( {2{x^2} - 1} \right) = 2{\left( 3 \right)^2} - 1 = 18 - 1 = 17{/tex}

And {tex}f\left( 3 \right) = 2{\left( 3 \right)^2} - 1 = 18 - 1 = 17{/tex}

Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f\left( x \right) = f\left( x \right),{/tex} therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 3.

question_html: Examine the function for continuity. f(x) = x - 5
solution_text: The given function is f(x) = x - 5 We know that f is defined at every real number k and its value at k is k - 5. We can see that {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x - 5) = k - 5 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {{f}}({{k}}){/tex} Therefore, f is continuous at every real number and thus, it is a continuous function.
solution_html:

Therefore, f is continuous at every real number and thus, it is a continuous function.

question_html:
Examine the function for continuity.

solution_text:
The given function is {tex}f(x)= rac{x^{2}-25}{x+5}, x 
eq 5{/tex} For any real number {tex}k 
eq 5{/tex} , we get, {tex}\lim _{x ightarrow \mathbf{k}} f(x)=\lim _{x ightarrow k}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x+5}=\lim _{x ightarrow k}  rac{(x-5)(x+5)}{x+5}=\lim _{x ightarrow k}(x-5)=(k-5){/tex} Also, {tex}f(k) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x 	o k}  rac{{(k - 5)(k + 5)}}{{k + 5}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x 	o k} (k - 5) = (k - 5)({	ext{ As }}k 
e 5){/tex} Thus, {tex}\lim _{{x} ightarrow {k}} {f}({x})={f}({k}){/tex} Therefore, f is continuous at every point in the domain of f and thus, it is continuous function.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-25}{x+5}, x \neq 5{/tex} 
For any real number {tex}k \neq 5{/tex}, we get,
{tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow \mathbf{k}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow k} \frac{x^{2}-25}{x+5}=\lim _{x \rightarrow k} \frac{(x-5)(x+5)}{x+5}=\lim _{x \rightarrow k}(x-5)=(k-5){/tex} 
Also, {tex}f(k) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} \frac{{(k - 5)(k + 5)}}{{k + 5}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (k - 5) = (k - 5)({\text{ As }}k \ne 5){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\lim _{{x} \rightarrow {k}} {f}({x})={f}({k}){/tex} 
Therefore, f is continuous at every point in the domain of f and thus, it is continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Examine the function for continuity. f(x) = |x – 5|
question_html: Examine the function for continuity. f(x) = |x – 5|
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x)=|x-5|=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {5-x \text { , if } x<5} \\ {x-5, \text { if } x \geq 5} \end{array}\right.{/tex} The function f is defined at all points of the real line. Let k be the point on a real line. Then, we have 3 cases i.e. , k < 5, or k = 5 or k >5 Now, Case I: k<5 Then, f(k) = 5 – k {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (5 - x){/tex} = 5 – k = f(k) Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all real number less than 5. Case II: k = 5 Then, f(k) = f(5) = 5 – 5 = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} (5 - x){/tex} = 5 – 5 = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} (x - 5){/tex} = 5 – 5 = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at x = 5. Case III: k > 5 Then, f(k) = k – 5 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x - 5) = k - 5 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all real number greater than 5. Therefore, f is a continuous function.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=|x-5|=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {5-x \text { , if } x<5} \\ {x-5, \text { if } x \geq 5} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
The function f is defined at all points of the real line.
Let k be the point on a real line.
Then, we have 3 cases i.e. , k < 5, or k = 5 or k >5
Now,
Case I: k<5
Then, f(k) = 5 – k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (5 - x){/tex} = 5 – k = f(k)
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at all real number less than 5.
Case II: k = 5
Then, f(k) = f(5) = 5 – 5 = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} (5 - x){/tex}= 5 – 5 = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 5} (x - 5){/tex} = 5 – 5 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at x = 5.
Case III: k > 5
Then, f(k) = k – 5
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x - 5) = k - 5 = f(k){/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at all real number greater than 5.
Therefore, f is a continuous function.

question_text: Prove that the function {tex}f(x) = {x^n}{/tex} is continuous at x = n where n is a positive integer.
question_html: Prove that the function {tex}f(x) = {x^n}{/tex} is continuous at x = n where n is a positive integer.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f(x) = {x^n}{/tex} where n is a positive integer. Continuity at x = n, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to n} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to n} \left( {{x^n}} \right) = {n^n}{/tex} And {tex}f\left( n \right) = {n^n}{/tex} Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to n} f\left( x \right) = f\left( x \right){/tex} , therefore, {tex}f\left( x \right){/tex} is continuous at x = n.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f(x) = {x^n}{/tex} where n is a positive integer.

Continuity at x = n, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to n} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to n} \left( {{x^n}} \right) = {n^n}{/tex} 

And {tex}f\left( n \right) = {n^n}{/tex}

Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to n} f\left( x \right) = f\left( x \right){/tex}, therefore, {tex}f\left( x \right){/tex} is continuous at x = n.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Is the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} continuous at x = 0? At x = 1? At x = 2?
question_html:
Is the function f defined by 
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}  
continuous at x = 0? At x = 1? At x = 2?
solution_text: It is given that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x \text { . if } x \leq 1} \\ {5, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Case I : x = 0 We can see that f is defined at 0 and its value at 0 is 0. Left Hand Limit {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f(0 - h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - h = 0{/tex} Right Hand Limit {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f(0 + h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h = 0{/tex} Left Hand Limit = Right Hand Limit = f(0) Hence, f is continuous at x = 0. Case II : x = 1 We can see that f is defined at 1 and its value at 1 is 1. For x < 1 f(x) = x Hence, Left Hand Limit: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} x = 1{/tex} For x > 1 f(x) = 5 therefore, Right Hand Limit {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (5) = 5{/tex} Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1. Case III : x = 2 As, We can see that f is defined at 2 and its value at 2 is 5 Left Hand Limit: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f(2 - h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 5 = 5{/tex} Here f(2 - h) = 5, as h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Right Hand Limit : {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{x_ + }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to + \infty } f(2 + h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 5 = 5{/tex} Left Hand Limit = Right Hand Limit = f(2) Here f(2 + h) = 5, as h h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Hence, f is continuous at x = 2.
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x \text { . if } x \leq 1} \\ {5, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
Case I: x = 0
We can see that f is defined at 0 and its value at 0 is 0.
Left Hand Limit {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f(0 - h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - h = 0{/tex} 
Right Hand Limit {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f(0 + h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} h = 0{/tex} 
Left Hand Limit = Right Hand Limit = f(0) Hence, f is continuous  at x = 0.
Case II: x = 1
We can see that f is defined at 1 and its value at 1 is 1.
For x < 1
f(x) = x Hence, Left Hand Limit:
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} x = 1{/tex} 
For x > 1 f(x) = 5 therefore, Right Hand Limit
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (5) = 5{/tex} 
Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1.
Case III: x = 2
As, We can see that f is defined at 2 and its value at 2 is 5
Left Hand Limit:
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f(2 - h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 5 = 5{/tex} 
Here f(2 - h) = 5, as h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Right Hand Limit : 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{x_ + }} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to + \infty } f(2 + h){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 5 = 5{/tex} 
Left Hand Limit = Right Hand Limit = f(2)
Here f(2 + h) = 5, as h h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 - h {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Hence, f is continuous at x = 2.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 2x + 3,\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ 2x - 3,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 2x + 3,\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ 2x - 3,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 2x + 3,\,\,\,if\,\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ 2x - 3,\,\,if\,\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} Here f(x) is defined for {tex}x \leq 2{/tex} i.e., on {tex}\left( { - \infty ,2} \right]{/tex} and also for {tex}x > 2{/tex} i.e., on {tex}\left( {2,\infty } \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f is {tex}\left( { - \infty ,2} \right] \cup \left( {2,\infty } \right) = \left( { - \infty ,\infty } \right) = R{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} For all {tex}x < 2,f\left( x \right) = 2x + 3{/tex} is a polynomial and hence continuous and for all {tex}x > 2,f\left( x \right) = 2x - 3{/tex} is a continuous and hence f(x) is continuous on R – {2}. Now Left Hand limit at x=2 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \left( {2x + 3} \right) = 2 \times 2 + 3 = 4 + 3 = 7{/tex} Right Hand limit at x=2 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {2x - 3} \right) = 2 \times 2 - 3 = 4 - 3 = 1{/tex} Since {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f\left( x \right) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f\left( x \right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2}} f\left( x \right){/tex} does not exist and hence f(x) is discontinuous at x =2 (only)
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 2x + 3,\,\,\,if\,\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ 2x - 3,\,\,if\,\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

Here f(x) is defined for {tex}x \leq 2{/tex} i.e., on {tex}\left( { - \infty ,2} \right]{/tex} and also for {tex}x > 2{/tex} i.e., on {tex}\left( {2,\infty } \right){/tex} 

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f is {tex}\left( { - \infty ,2} \right] \cup \left( {2,\infty } \right) = \left( { - \infty ,\infty } \right) = R{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} For all {tex}x < 2,f\left( x \right) = 2x + 3{/tex} is a polynomial and hence continuous and for all {tex}x > 2,f\left( x \right) = 2x - 3{/tex} is a continuous and hence f(x) is  continuous on R – {2}.

Now Left Hand limit  at x=2{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \left( {2x + 3} \right) = 2 \times 2 + 3 = 4 + 3 = 7{/tex}

Right Hand limit at x=2{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {2x - 3} \right) = 2 \times 2 - 3 = 4 - 3 = 1{/tex}

Since {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f\left( x \right) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f\left( x \right){/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2}} f\left( x \right){/tex} does not exist and hence f(x) is discontinuous at x =2 (only)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {|x| + 3,}&{if}&{x \leq - 3} \\ { - 2x,}&{if}&{ - 3 < x < 3} \\ {6x + 2}&{if}&{x \geq 3} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {|x| + 3,}&{if}&{x \leq - 3} \\ { - 2x,}&{if}&{ - 3 < x < 3} \\ {6x + 2}&{if}&{x \geq 3} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
solution_text: At x = -3, f(-3) = |-3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} f(x){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} - 2x{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2( - 3 + h){/tex} = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x)= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3^-} |x+3| = 6{/tex} Hence continuous at x = -3 At x = 3, {tex}f(3) = 6 \times 3 + 2 = 20{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} ( - 2x){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2(3 - h){/tex} = - 6 {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} (6x + 2){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} [6(3 + h) + 2]{/tex} = 20 {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x){/tex} Hence f(x) is not continuous at x = 3
solution_html:
At x = -3,

f(-3) = |-3| + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} f(x){/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {-3^ + }} - 2x{/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2( - 3 + h){/tex} = 6

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x)= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3^-} |x+3| = 6{/tex}

Hence continuous at x = -3

At x = 3,

{tex}f(3) = 6 \times 3 + 2 = 20{/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} ( - 2x){/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - 2(3 - h){/tex} = - 6

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} (6x + 2){/tex}

{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} [6(3 + h) + 2]{/tex} = 20

{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {3^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x){/tex}

Hence f(x) is not  continuous at x = 3

question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\left| x \right|}}{x},\,\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\left| x \right|}}{x},\,\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\left| x \right|}}{x},\,\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac{x}{x} = 1{/tex} if x > 0 and {tex}\frac{{ - x}}{x} = - 1{/tex} if x < 0. {tex} \Rightarrow f\left( x \right) = 1{/tex} if x > 0, {tex}f(x) = - 1{/tex} if x < 0 and f(x) = 0 if x = 0 It is clear that domain of f(x) is R as f(x) is defined for x > 0, x < 0 and x = 0. For all {tex}x > 0,f\left( x \right) = 1{/tex} is a constant function and hence continuous. For all {tex}x < 0,f\left( x \right) = - 1{/tex} is a constant function and hence continuous. Therefore f(x) is continuous on R – {0}. Now Left Hand limit at x=0 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \left( { - 1} \right) = - 1{/tex} Right Hand limit at x=0 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( 1 \right) = 1{/tex} Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f\left( x \right){/tex} Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right){/tex} does not exist and hence f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0 (only).
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\left| x \right|}}{x},\,\,if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac{x}{x} = 1{/tex} if x > 0 and {tex}\frac{{ - x}}{x} = - 1{/tex} if x < 0. 

{tex} \Rightarrow f\left( x \right) = 1{/tex} if x > 0, {tex}f(x) = - 1{/tex} if x < 0 and f(x) = 0 if x = 0 

It is clear that domain of f(x) is R as f(x) is defined for x > 0, x < 0 and x = 0.

For all {tex}x > 0,f\left( x \right) = 1{/tex} is a constant function and hence continuous.

For all {tex}x < 0,f\left( x \right) = - 1{/tex} is a constant function and hence continuous.

Therefore f(x) is continuous on R – {0}.

Now Left Hand limit at x=0 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \left( { - 1} \right) = - 1{/tex}

Right Hand limit at x=0  {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( 1 \right) = 1{/tex}

Since {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f\left( x \right){/tex} 

Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right){/tex} does not exist and hence f(x) is discontinuous at x = 0 (only).
