question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {\frac{x}{|x|},} & {\text { if } x<0} \\ {-1,} & {\text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {\frac{x}{|x|},} & {\text { if } x<0} \\ {-1,} & {\text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {\frac{x}{|x|}, \text { if } x<0} \\ {-1, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} We know that if x < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x| = -x So, we can rewrite the given function as: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x}{|x|}=& \frac{x}{-x}=-1 , \text { if } x<0 \\ &-1, \text { if } x \geq 0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -1 for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R Let k be the point on a real line. Then, f(k) = -1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\text{k}}} ( - 1){/tex} =-1= f(k) Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} = f(k) Therefore, the given function is a continuous function.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {\frac{x}{|x|}, \text { if } x<0} \\ {-1, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
We know that if x < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |x| = -x
So, we can rewrite the given function as:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x}{|x|}=& \frac{x}{-x}=-1 , \text { if } x<0 \\ &-1, \text { if } x \geq 0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = -1 for all x {tex}\in{/tex} R
Let k be the point on a real line.
Then, f(k) = -1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\text{k}}} ( - 1){/tex} =-1= f(k)
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} = f(k)
Therefore, the given function is a continuous function.

question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 1 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,x < 1 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 1 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,x < 1 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 1 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,x < 1 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} It is observed that f(x) being polynomial is continuous for {tex}x >1{/tex} and x < 1 . {tex}{/tex} Continuity at x = 1, R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} \left( {x + 1} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {1 + h + 1} \right) = 2{/tex} L.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {{{\left( {1 - h} \right)}^2} + 1} \right) = 2{/tex} And f(1) = 2 Since L.H.L. = R.H.L. = f(1) Therefore, f(x) is a continuous at x = 1 . {tex}{/tex} Hence, f(x) has no point of discontinuity.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 1 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,x < 1 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

It is observed that f(x) being polynomial is continuous for {tex}x >1{/tex} and x < 1 . {tex}{/tex}

Continuity at x = 1, R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} \left( {x + 1} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {1 + h + 1} \right) = 2{/tex} 

L.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {{{\left( {1 - h} \right)}^2} + 1} \right) = 2{/tex}

And f(1) = 2 

Since L.H.L. = R.H.L. = f(1) 

Therefore, f(x) is a continuous at x = 1 .{tex}{/tex}

Hence, f(x) has no point of discontinuity.

question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^3} - 3,\,\,if\,\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^3} - 3,\,\,if\,\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^3} - 3,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 2 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} If x<2,then f(x) being a polynomial is Continuous and if x>2 f(x) being polynomial is Continuous. At x = 2, L.H.L. {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \left( {{x^3} - 3} \right) = 8 - 3 = 5{/tex} R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right) = 4 + 1 = 5{/tex} {tex}f\left( 2 \right) = {2^3} - 3 = 8 - 3 = 5{/tex} Since L.H.L. = R.H.L. = f(2) Therefore, f(x) is a continuous at x = 2. {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Therefore, f(x) is a continuous for all x {tex}\in R{/tex} . Hence the function f(x) has no point of discontinuity.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^3} - 3,\,\,if\,\,x \leqslant 2 \hfill \\ {x^2} + 1,\,\,if\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}

If x<2,then f(x) being a polynomial is Continuous and if x>2 f(x) being polynomial is Continuous.

At x = 2, L.H.L. {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} \left( {{x^3} - 3} \right) = 8 - 3 = 5{/tex} 

R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right) = 4 + 1 = 5{/tex}

{tex}f\left( 2 \right) = {2^3} - 3 = 8 - 3 = 5{/tex}

Since   L.H.L. = R.H.L. = f(2) 

Therefore, f(x) is a continuous at x = 2. {tex}{/tex}

{tex}{/tex}Therefore, f(x) is a continuous for all x{tex}\in R{/tex}.

Hence the function f(x) has no point of discontinuity.

question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x^{10}-1,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x^{2},} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where f is defined by:{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x^{10}-1,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x^{2},} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x^{10}-1 . \text { if } x \leq 1 \\ x^{2}, \text { if } x>1 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} The function f is defined at all points of the real line. Let k be the point on a real line. Then, we have 3 cases i.e., k < 1, or k = 1 or k > 1 Now, Case I : k > 1 Then, f(k) = k 10 - 1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} \left( {{x^{10}} - 1} \right) = {k^{10}} - 1 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all real number less than 1. Case II: k = 1 Then, f(k) = f(1) = 1 10 - 1 = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} \left( {{x^{10}} - 1} \right) = {1^{10}} - 1 = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} \left( {{x^2}} \right) = {1^2} = 1{/tex} Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1. Case III : k > 1 Then, f(k) = 1 2 = 1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} \left( {{x^2}} \right) = {1^2} = 1 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all real number greater than 1. Therefore, x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x^{10}-1 . \text { if } x \leq 1 \\ x^{2}, \text { if } x>1 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} 
The function f is defined at all points of the real line.
Let k be the point on a real line.
Then, we have 3 cases i.e., k < 1, or k = 1 or k > 1
Now,
Case I: k > 1
Then, f(k) = k10 - 1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} \left( {{x^{10}} - 1} \right) = {k^{10}} - 1 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous at all real number less than 1.
Case II: k = 1
Then, f(k) = f(1) = 110 - 1 = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} \left( {{x^{10}} - 1} \right) = {1^{10}} - 1 = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} \left( {{x^2}} \right) = {1^2} = 1{/tex} 
Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1.
Case III: k > 1
Then, f(k) = 12 = 1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} \left( {{x^2}} \right) = {1^2} = 1 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous at all real number greater than 1.
Therefore, x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.

question_text: Is the function defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x+5,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x-5,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} a continuous function?
question_html:
Is the function defined by 
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {x+5,} & {\text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x-5,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
a continuous function?
solution_text: The given function is f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x+5, \text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x-5, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} The function f is defined at all points of the real line. Let k be the point on a real line. Then, we have 3 cases i.e., k < 1, or k = 1 or k > 1 Now, Case I : k < 1 Then, f(k) = k + 5 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 5) = k + 5 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} f(x) = f(k){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all real number less than 1. Case II : k = 1 Then, f(k) = f(1) = 1 + 5 = 6 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (x + 5) = 1 + 5 = 6{/tex} {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}(x-5)=1-5=-4{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1. Case III : k > 1 Then, f(k) = k - 5 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} (x - 5) = k - 5{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all real number greater than 1. Therefore, x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.
solution_html:
The given function is f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} {x+5, \text { if } x \leq 1} \\ {x-5, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
The function f is defined at all points of the real line.
Let k be the point on a real line.
Then, we have 3 cases i.e., k < 1, or k = 1 or k > 1
Now, 
Case I: k < 1
Then, f(k) = k + 5
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 5) = k + 5 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} f(x) = f(k){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at all real number less than 1.
Case II: k = 1
Then, f(k) = f(1) = 1 + 5 = 6
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (x + 5) = 1 + 5 = 6{/tex} 
{tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}(x-5)=1-5=-4{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1.
Case III: k > 1
Then, f(k) = k - 5
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} (x - 5) = k - 5{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous at all real number greater than 1.
Therefore, x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.

question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {3,} & {\text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4,} & {\text { if } 1<x<3} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } 3 \leq x \leq 10} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {3,} & {\text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4,} & {\text { if } 1<x<3} \\ {5,} & {\text { if } 3 \leq x \leq 10} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {3, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4, \text { if } 1<x<3} \\ {5, \text { if } 3 \leq x \leq 10} \end{array}\right.{/tex} The function f is defined at all points of interval [0, 10] Let k be the point in the interval [0, 10] Then, we have 5 cases i.e., 0 {tex}\le{/tex} k < 1,k = 1, 1 < k < 3, k = 3 or 3 < k {tex}\le{/tex} 10. Now, Case I : {tex}0 \leq k<1{/tex} Then, f(k) = 3 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (3) = 3 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} f(x) = f(k){/tex} Hence, f is continuous in the interval [0, 10). Case II : k = 1 f(1) = 3 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (3) = 3{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (4) = 4{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x, + } {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1 Case III : 1 < k < 3 Then, f(k) = 4 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (4) = 4 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous in (1, 3). Case IV : k = 3 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} (4) = 4{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} (5) = 5{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} Hence, f is not continuous at x = 3. Case V : 3 < k {tex}\le{/tex} 10 Then, f(x) = 5 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (5) = 5 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {{f}}({{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all points of the interval (3, 10]. Therefore, x = 1 and 3 are the points of discontinuity of f.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {3, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4, \text { if } 1<x<3} \\ {5, \text { if } 3 \leq x \leq 10} \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
The function f is defined at all points of interval [0, 10]
Let k be the point in the interval [0, 10]
Then, we have 5 cases i.e., 0 {tex}\le{/tex} k < 1,k = 1, 1 < k < 3, k = 3 or 3 < k {tex}\le{/tex} 10.
Now, Case I: {tex}0 \leq k<1{/tex} 
Then, f(k) = 3
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (3) = 3 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} f(x) = f(k){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous in the interval [0, 10).
Case II: k = 1
f(1) = 3
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (3) = 3{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (4) = 4{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x, + } {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1
Case III: 1 < k < 3
Then, f(k) = 4
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (4) = 4 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous in (1, 3).
Case IV: k = 3
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ - }} (4) = 4{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {3^ + }} (5) = 5{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is not continuous at x = 3.
Case V: 3 < k {tex}\le{/tex} 10
Then, f(x) = 5
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (5) = 5 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {{f}}({{k}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous at all points of the interval (3, 10].
Therefore, x = 1 and 3 are the points of discontinuity of f.

question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {2 x,} & {\text { if } x<0} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4 x,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f, where f is defined by: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {2 x,} & {\text { if } x<0} \\ {0,} & {\text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4 x,} & {\text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x, \text { if } x \leq 0} \\ {0, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4 x, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} The function f is defined at all points of the real line. Then, we have 5 cases i.e., k < 0, k = 0, 0 < k < 1, k = 1, k > 1. Now, Case I : k < 0 Then, f(k) = 2k {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} (2x) = 2k = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {{f}}({{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all points x such that x < 0 Case II : k = 0 f(0) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} (2x) = 2 \times 0 = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (0) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow \mathbf{0}^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\lim _{x \rightarrow \mathrm{0}^{+}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{k}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at x = 0. Case III : 0 < k < 1 Then, f(k) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (0) = 0 = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} Hence, f is continuous in (0, 1) Case IV : k = 1 Then f(k) = f(1) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (0) = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (4x) = 4 \times 1 = 4{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1 Case V : k > 1 Then, f(k) = 4k {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (4x) = 4k = f(k){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {\mathbf{f}}({{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all point x, s.t. x > 1 Therefore, x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x, \text { if } x \leq 0} \\ {0, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {4 x, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
The function f is defined at all points of the real line.
Then, we have 5 cases i.e., k < 0, k = 0, 0 < k < 1, k = 1, k > 1.
Now, Case I: k < 0
Then, f(k) = 2k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} (2x) = 2k = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {{f}}({{k}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous at all points x such that x < 0
Case II: k = 0
f(0) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} (2x) = 2 \times 0 = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (0) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow \mathbf{0}^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\lim _{x \rightarrow \mathrm{0}^{+}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{k}){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at x = 0.
Case III: 0 < k < 1
Then, f(k) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (0) = 0 = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous in (0, 1)
Case IV: k = 1
Then f(k) = f(1) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (0) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (4x) = 4 \times 1 = 4{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is not continuous at x = 1
Case V: k > 1
Then, f(k) = 4k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (4x) = 4k = f(k){/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{{x}} \to {{k}}} {{f}}({{x}}) = {\mathbf{f}}({{k}}){/tex} 
Hence, f is continuous at all point x, s.t. x > 1
Therefore, x = 1 is the only point of discontinuity of f.

question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f, where f is defined by: {tex}[f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} { - 2,\ {\rm{ if }} \ x \le - 1}\\ { 2x, \ \ {\rm{ if }} - 1 < x \le 1}\\ {{\rm{\ \ \ 2, \ if }} \ x > 1} \end{array}} \right.]{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f, where f is defined by: {tex}[f(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} { - 2,\ {\rm{ if }} \ x \le - 1}\\ { 2x, \ \ {\rm{ if }} - 1 < x \le 1}\\ {{\rm{\ \ \ 2, \ if }} \ x > 1} \end{array}} \right.]{/tex}
solution_text: The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {-2, \text { if } x \leq-1} \\ {2 x, \text { if }-1 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {2, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex} The function f is defined at all points of the real line. Then, we have 5 cases i.e., k < -1, k = -1, -1 < k < 1, k = 1 or k > 1. Now, Case I: k < 0 Then, f(k) = -2 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\text{k}}} ( - 2){/tex} = -2= f(k) Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) {/tex} = f(k) Hence, f is continuous at all points x, s.t. x < -1. Case II: k = -1 f(k) = f(=1) = -2 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ - }} {/tex} (-2) = -2 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ + }} {/tex} (2x) = 2 × (-1) = -2 {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at x = -1. Case III: -1 < k < 1 Then, f(k) = 2k {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} (2x) = 2k = f(k){/tex} Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex} Hence, f is continuous in (-1, 1). Case IV: k = 1 Then f(k) = f(1) = 2 × 1 = 2 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (2x){/tex} = 2 × 1 = 2 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (2) = 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {k^ + }} f(x) = f(x){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at x = 1. Case V: k > 1 Then, f(k) = 2 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (2) = 2 = f(k){/tex} Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Hence, f is continuous at all points x, s.t. x > 1. Therefore, f is continuous at all points of the real line.
solution_html:
The given function is {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {-2, \text { if } x \leq-1} \\ {2 x, \text { if }-1 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {2, \text { if } x>1} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
The function f is defined at all points of the real line.
Then, we have 5 cases i.e., k < -1, k = -1, -1 < k < 1, k = 1 or k > 1.
Now,
Case I: k < 0
Then, f(k) = -2
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\text{k}}} ( - 2){/tex}= -2= f(k)
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) {/tex} = f(k)
Hence, f is continuous at all points x, s.t. x < -1.
Case II: k = -1
f(k) = f(=1) = -2
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ - }} {/tex}(-2) = -2
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ + }} {/tex}(2x) = 2 × (-1) = -2
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {{\text{k}}^ + }} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at x = -1.
Case III: -1 < k < 1
Then, f(k) = 2k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} (2x) = 2k = f(k){/tex}
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = f(k){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous in (-1, 1).
Case IV: k = 1
Then f(k) = f(1) = 2 × 1 = 2
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} (2x){/tex}  = 2 × 1 = 2
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (2) = 2{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {{\mathbf{k}}^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {k^ + }} f(x) = f(x){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at x = 1.
Case V: k > 1
Then, f(k) = 2
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (2) = 2 = f(k){/tex}
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Hence, f is continuous at all points x, s.t. x > 1.
Therefore, f is continuous at all points of the real line.

question_text: Find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3.
question_html: Find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = 3.
solution_text: {tex}{/tex} According to the question,we have to find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3. Therefore , LHL = RHL = f(3).........(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } ( a x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 3 - h ) + 1 ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 3a + 1 and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( b x + 3 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ b ( 3 + h + 3 ]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 b + b h + 3 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 3b + 3 From Eq. (i), we have LHL = RHL {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 3 Therefore, 3a - 3b = 2, which is the required relation between a and b.
solution_html:
{tex}{/tex}According to the question,we have to find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = 3.

Therefore , LHL = RHL = f(3).........(i)
Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } ( a x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 3 - h ) + 1 ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 3a + 1
and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( b x + 3 ){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ b ( 3 + h + 3 ]{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 b + b h + 3 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 3b + 3
From Eq. (i), we have
LHL = RHL{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}  3a + 1 = 3b + 3
Therefore, 3a - 3b = 2, which is the required relation between a and b.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: For what value of λ is the function defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {\lambda\left(x^{2}-2 x\right),} & {\text { if } x \leq 0} \\ {4 x+1,} & {\text { if } x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} continuous at x = 0? What about continuity at x = 1?
question_html: For what value of λ is the function defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {\lambda\left(x^{2}-2 x\right),} & {\text { if } x \leq 0} \\ {4 x+1,} & {\text { if } x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} continuous at x = 0? What about continuity at x = 1?
solution_text: It is given that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {\lambda\left(x^{2}-2 x\right), \text { if } x \leq 0} \\ {4 x+1, \text { if } x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Let f be continuous at x = 0, then, we get {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = f(0){/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \left( {\lambda \left( {{x^2} - 2x} \right)} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \left( {\lambda \left( {{0^2} - 2 \times 0} \right)} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (4x + 1){/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 1 = 1 {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x){/tex} Therefore, there is no value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which f is continuous at x = 0 f(1) = 4x + 1 = 4 × 1 + 1 = 5 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} (4x + 1){/tex} = 4 × 1 + 1 = 5 Then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to 1} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}(1){/tex} Therefore, for any values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , f is continuous at x = 1
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{c} {\lambda\left(x^{2}-2 x\right), \text { if } x \leq 0} \\ {4 x+1, \text { if } x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Let f be continuous at x = 0, then, we get
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = f(0){/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \left( {\lambda \left( {{x^2} - 2x} \right)} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \left( {\lambda \left( {{0^2} - 2 \times 0} \right)} \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (4x + 1){/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + 1 = 1
{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) \ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x){/tex}
Therefore, there is no value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which f is continuous at x = 0
f(1) = 4x + 1 = 4 × 1 + 1 = 5
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} (4x + 1){/tex} = 4 × 1 + 1 = 5
Then {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to 1} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{f}}(1){/tex}
Therefore, for any values of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, f is continuous at x = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function defined by {tex}g(x) = x - \left[ x \right]{/tex} is discontinuous at all integral points. Here [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x.
question_html: Show that the function defined by {tex}g(x) = x - \left[ x \right]{/tex} is discontinuous at all integral points. Here [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x.
solution_text: Let n {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}I{/tex} Then, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^- }}[x]=n-1{/tex} {tex}\therefore [x]=n-1\ \forall \ x\in [n-1,n] {/tex} and g(n) = n - n = 0 {tex}[\therefore [n]=n\ because\ n\in I]{/tex} Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^-}} g(x)=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^-}}(x-[x])=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^- }}x-\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^- }}[x]{/tex} {tex}=n-(n-1)=1{/tex} Also, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^+ }}g(x)=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }}(x-[x]){/tex} {tex}=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }}x-\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }}[x]=n-n=0{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ - }}g(x)\ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }} g(x){/tex} Hence, g(x) is discontinuous at all integral points.
solution_html:
Let n {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}I{/tex}
Then,{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^- }}[x]=n-1{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore [x]=n-1\ \forall \ x\in [n-1,n] {/tex}
and g(n) = n - n = 0  {tex}[\therefore [n]=n\ because\ n\in I]{/tex}
Now, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^-}} g(x)=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^-}}(x-[x])=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^- }}x-\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^- }}[x]{/tex}
{tex}=n-(n-1)=1{/tex}
Also, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^+ }}g(x)=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }}(x-[x]){/tex}
{tex}=\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }}x-\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }}[x]=n-n=0{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ - }}g(x)\ne \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {n^ + }} g(x){/tex}
Hence, g(x) is discontinuous at all integral points.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Is the function defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2} - \sin x + 5{/tex} continuous at {tex}x = \pi {/tex} ?
question_html: Is the function defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2} - \sin x + 5{/tex} continuous at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}?
solution_text: Given: {tex}f(x) = {x^2} - \sin x + 5{/tex} L.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {{x^2} - \sin x + 5} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {{{\left( {\pi - h} \right)}^2} - \sin \left( {\pi - h} \right) + 5} \right] = {\pi ^2} + 5{/tex} R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \left( {{x^2} - \sin x + 5} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {{{\left( {\pi + h} \right)}^2} - \sin \left( {\pi + h} \right) + 5} \right] = {\pi ^2} + 5{/tex} And {tex}f\left( \pi \right) = {\pi ^2} - \sin \pi + 5 = {\pi ^2} + 5{/tex} Since L.H.L. = R.H.L. = {tex}f\left( \pi \right){/tex} Therefore, f is continuous at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f(x) = {x^2} - \sin x + 5{/tex} 

L.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {{x^2} - \sin x + 5} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {{{\left( {\pi - h} \right)}^2} - \sin \left( {\pi - h} \right) + 5} \right] = {\pi ^2} + 5{/tex} 

R.H.L.{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \left( {{x^2} - \sin x + 5} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {{{\left( {\pi + h} \right)}^2} - \sin \left( {\pi + h} \right) + 5} \right] = {\pi ^2} + 5{/tex}  

And {tex}f\left( \pi \right) = {\pi ^2} - \sin \pi + 5 = {\pi ^2} + 5{/tex} 

Since L.H.L. = R.H.L. = {tex}f\left( \pi \right){/tex} 

Therefore, f is continuous at  {tex}x = \pi {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function: f(x) = sin x + cos x
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function: f(x) = sin x + cos x
solution_text: We know that if g and k are two continuous functions, then, g + k, g – k and g.k are also continuous. First we have to prove that g(x) = sin x and k(x) = cos x are continuous functions. Now, let g(x) = sinx We know that g(x) = sinx is defined for every real number. Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 g(h) = sinh {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} \sin x{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \sin (h + k){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} [\sinh \cos k + \cosh \sin k]{/tex} = sin h.cos 0 + cos h.sin 0 = sinh + 0 = sin h Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = g(h){/tex} Therefore, g is a continuous function ...(1) Now, let k(x) = cos x We know that k(x) = cos x is defined for every real number. Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 Now k(h) = cosh {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} {\text{k}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{h}}} \cos {\text{x}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{k}} \to 0} \cos ({\text{h}} + {\text{k}}){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} [\cosh \cos k - \sinh \sin k]{/tex} = cos h.cos 0 - sin h.sin 0 = cos h - 0 = cos h Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} k(x) = k(h){/tex} Therefore, k is a continuous function ...(2) So, from (1) and (2), we get, f(x) = g(x) + k(x) = sinx + cosx is a continuous function.
solution_html:
We know that if g and k are two continuous functions, then,
g + k, g – k and g.k are also continuous.
First we have to prove that g(x) = sin x and k(x) = cos x are continuous functions.
Now, let g(x) = sinx
We know that g(x) = sinx is defined for every real number.
Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k
So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
g(h) = sinh
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} \sin x{/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \sin (h + k){/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} [\sinh \cos k + \cosh \sin k]{/tex}
= sin h.cos 0 + cos h.sin 0
= sinh + 0
= sin h
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = g(h){/tex}
Therefore, g is a continuous function ...(1)
Now, let k(x) = cos x
We know that k(x) = cos x is defined for every real number.
Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k
So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
Now k(h) = cosh
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} {\text{k}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{h}}} \cos {\text{x}}{/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{k}} \to 0} \cos ({\text{h}} + {\text{k}}){/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} [\cosh \cos k - \sinh \sin k]{/tex}
= cos h.cos 0 - sin h.sin 0
= cos h - 0
= cos h
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} k(x) = k(h){/tex}
Therefore, k is a continuous function ...(2)
So, from (1) and (2), we get,
f(x) = g(x) + k(x) = sinx + cosx is a continuous function.

question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function: f(x) = sin x - cos x
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function: f(x) = sin x - cos x
solution_text: We known that if g and r are two continuous functions, then g + r, g – r and g.r are also continuous. First we have to prove that g(x) = sinx and r(x) = cosx are continuous functions. Now, let g(x) = sinx We know that g(x) = sinx is defined for every real number. Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 g(h) = sin h {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} \sin x{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{k \to 0} \sin (h + k){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{k \to 0} [\sinh \cos k + \cosh \sin k]{/tex} = sinh.cos0 + cosh.sin0 = sinh + 0 = sin h Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = g(h){/tex} Therefore, g is a continuous function …(1) Now, let f(x) = cos x We know that f(x) = cos x is defined for every real number. Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 Now f(h) = cosh {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{h}}} \cos {\text{x}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{k}} \to 0} \cos ({\text{h}} + {\text{k}}){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} [\cosh \cos k - \sinh \sin k]{/tex} = coshcos0 - sinhsin0 = cosh - 0 = cosh Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} f(x) = f(h){/tex} Therefore, f is a continuous function ….(2) So, from (1) and (2), we get, r(x) = g(x) - f(x) = sinx - cosx is a continuous function.
solution_html:
We known that if g and r are two continuous functions, then
g + r, g – r and g.r are also continuous.
First we have to prove that g(x) = sinx and r(x) = cosx are continuous functions.
Now, let g(x) = sinx
We know that g(x) = sinx is defined for every real number.
Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k
So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
g(h) = sin h
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} \sin x{/tex}
 = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{k \to 0} \sin (h + k){/tex}
 = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{k \to 0} [\sinh \cos k + \cosh \sin k]{/tex}
 = sinh.cos0 + cosh.sin0
 = sinh + 0
 = sin h
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} g(x) = g(h){/tex}
Therefore, g is a continuous function …(1)
Now, let f(x) = cos x
We know that f(x) = cos x is defined for every real number.
Let h be a real number. Now, put x = h + k
So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} h and k {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
Now f(h) = cosh
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{h}}} \cos {\text{x}}{/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{k}} \to 0} \cos ({\text{h}} + {\text{k}}){/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} [\cosh \cos k - \sinh \sin k]{/tex}
= coshcos0 - sinhsin0
= cosh - 0

Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to h} f(x) = f(h){/tex}
Therefore, f is a continuous function ….(2)
So, from (1) and (2), we get,
r(x) = g(x) - f(x) = sinx - cosx is a continuous function.

question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function: {tex}f(x) = \sin x.\cos x{/tex}
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function: {tex}f(x) = \sin x.\cos x{/tex}
solution_text: Since sin x and cos x are continuous functions and product of two continuous function is a continuous function, therefore {tex}f(x) = \sin x.\cos x{/tex} is a continuous function.
solution_html: Since sin x and cos x are continuous functions and product of two continuous function is a continuous function, therefore {tex}f(x) = \sin x.\cos x{/tex} is a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the cosine, cosecant, secant and cotangent functions.
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the cosine, cosecant, secant and cotangent functions.
solution_html:
We know that if g and h are two continuous functions, then
{tex}\frac{h(x)}{g(x)}, g(x) \neq{/tex} 0 is continuous
Clearly, {tex}\frac{1}{g(x)}, ~~g(x) \neq{/tex} 0 is continuous
So, first we have to prove that g(x) = sinx and h(x) = cosx are continuous functions.
Let g(x) = sin x
We know that g(x) = sin x is defined for every real number.
Let h be a real number. Now, put x = k + h
So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} k and h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
g(k) = sin k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} \sin x{/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \sin ({\text{k}} + {\text{h}}){/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ \ h \to 0} [\operatorname{sink cosh} + {\text{ cosk sinh }}]{/tex}
= sin k cos 0 + cos k sin 0
= sin k + 0
= sin k
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = g(k){/tex}
Therefore, g is a continuous function ...(1)
Let h(x) = {tex}cosx{/tex}
We know that h(x) = cos x is defined for every real number.
Let k be a real number. Now, put x = k + h
So, if x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} k and h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0
h(k) = sin k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} \cos {\text{x}}{/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \cos ({\text{k}} + {\text{h}}){/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} [\operatorname{cos k cos h} - \operatorname{sin k sin h} ]{/tex}
= cos k cos 0 - sin k sin 0
= cos k - 0
= cos k
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} ({\text{x}}) = {\text{h}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Therefore, g is a continuous function ...(2)
So, from (1) and (2), we get
cosec x = {tex}\frac{1}{\sin x}{/tex}, sinx {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 is continuous
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}cosec x=\frac{1}{sinx},~~~~ x \neq n \pi, (n\in Z){/tex} is continuous
Thus, cosecant is continuous except at {tex}x = np,{/tex}  {tex} [n \in Z]{/tex}
Now, {tex}sec x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\cos x}{/tex}, {tex}cosx \neq 0{/tex} is continuous
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}sec x, x \neq (2n + 1) \frac {\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}(\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z}){/tex} is continuous
Thus, secant is continuous except at x = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2},(n \in Z){/tex}
cot x = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{COSX}}{\sin \mathrm{X}}{/tex}, sin x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 is continuous
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot x, x {tex}\neq{/tex} n{tex}\pi{/tex} (n {tex}\in{/tex} Z) is continuous
Thus, cotangent is continuous except at x = np, (n {tex}\in{/tex} Z)

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\sin x}}{x},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} .
question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f, where {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\sin x}}{x},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\sin x}}{x},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} At x = 0, L.H.L. {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0 }} \frac{{\sin \left( x \right)}}{{ x}} = 1{/tex} R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0 }} \left( {x + 1} \right) = 0 + 1 = 1{/tex} f(0) = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 0. When x < 0,then f(x) is the ratio of two functions x and sinx which are both continuous, therefore {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{x}{/tex} is also continuous. When {tex}x > 0,f\left( x \right) = x + 1{/tex} is a polynomial, then f is continuous. Therefore, f is continuous at any point.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{\sin x}}{x},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ x + 1,\,\,if\,x \geq 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} 

At x = 0, L.H.L. {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0 }} \frac{{\sin \left( x \right)}}{{ x}} = 1{/tex} 

R.H.L. {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0 }} \left( {x + 1} \right) = 0 + 1 = 1{/tex}

f(0) = 1 

{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 0. 

When x < 0,then f(x) is the ratio of two functions x and sinx which are both  continuous, therefore {tex}\frac{{\sin x}}{x}{/tex} is also continuous.

When {tex}x > 0,f\left( x \right) = x + 1{/tex} is a polynomial, then f is continuous.

Therefore, f is continuous at any point.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Determine if f defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x},if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function?
question_html: Determine if f defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x},if\,x \ne 0 \hfill \\ 0,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function?
solution_text: Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x} = 0{/tex} x a finite quantity = 0 {tex}\left[ {\because \sin \frac{1}{x}{\text{lies between - 1 and 1}}} \right]{/tex} Also f(0) = 0 Since, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex} therefore, the function f is continuous at x = 0. Also,when {tex}x\ne0{/tex} ,then f(x) is the product of two continuous functions and hence Continuous.Hence,f(x) is continuous everywhere.
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {x^2}\sin \frac{1}{x} = 0{/tex} x a finite quantity = 0   

{tex}\left[ {\because \sin \frac{1}{x}{\text{lies between - 1 and 1}}} \right]{/tex}

Also f(0) = 0 

Since, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex} therefore, the function f is continuous at x = 0. 

Also,when {tex}x\ne0{/tex} ,then f(x) is the product of two continuous functions and hence Continuous.Hence,f(x) is continuous everywhere.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Examine the continuity of f, where f is defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {\sin x-\cos x,} & {\text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {-1,} & {\text { if } x=0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_html: Examine the continuity of f, where f is defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {\sin x-\cos x,} & {\text { if } x \neq 0} \\ {-1,} & {\text { if } x=0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \sin x-\cos x, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ -1, \text { if } x=0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
We know that f is defined at all points of the real line.
Let k be a real number.
Case I: k {tex}\neq{/tex} 0,
Then f(k) = sin k - cos k
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{f}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} (\sin {\text{x}} - \cos {\text{x}}) = \sin {\text{k}} - {\text{cosk}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} (x) = f(k){/tex}
Thus, f is continuous at all points 'x' such that x {tex}\neq{/tex} 0.
Case II: k = 0
Then f(k) = f(0) = -1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} {/tex}(sin x - cos x) = sin 0 - cos 0 = 0 - 1 = -1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} {/tex}(sin x - cos x) = sin 0 - cos 0 = 0 - 1 = -1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = f(0){/tex}
Therefore, f is continuous at x = 0.
Therefore, f has no point of discontinuity.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}},\,\,if\,x \ne \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ 3,\,\,if\,\,x = \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
question_html: Find the values of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}},\,\,if\,x \ne \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ 3,\,\,if\,\,x = \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}}{/tex} {tex}\because x \to \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \ne \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Put {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2} + h{/tex} where {tex}h \to 0{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}}{{\pi - 2\left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - k\sinh }}{{\pi - \pi - 2h}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\sin \,h}}{{ 2h}} = \frac{k}{2} \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sin h}}{h}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{k}{2}{/tex} …….(i) And {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = 3{/tex} ……….(ii) {tex}\because f\left( x \right) = 3{/tex} when {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} [Given] Since f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{k}{2} = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = 6{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}}{/tex}

{tex}\because x \to \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x \ne \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

Put {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2} + h{/tex} where {tex}h \to 0{/tex} , we get

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) {/tex}{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}}{{\pi - 2\left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - k\sinh }}{{\pi - \pi - 2h}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\sin \,h}}{{ 2h}} = \frac{k}{2} \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sin h}}{h}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{k}{2}{/tex}…….(i)

And {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = 3{/tex} ……….(ii)

{tex}\because f\left( x \right) = 3{/tex} when {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} [Given]

Since  f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) and (ii),

{tex}\frac{k}{2} = 3{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow k = 6{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} k{x^2},\,\,if\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ 3,\,\,if\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
question_html: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} k{x^2},\,\,if\,x \leq 2 \hfill \\ 3,\,\,if\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 2.
solution_text: Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} k{x^2} = k \times {2^2}=4k{/tex} Since f(x) is continuous at x = 2 Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = f\left( 2 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4k = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} k{x^2} = k \times {2^2}=4k{/tex}
Since f(x) is continuous at x = 2 
Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = f\left( 2 \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4k = 3{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow k = \frac{3}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} Kx + 1\,\,if\,x \leq \pi \, \hfill \\ \cos x\,\,if\,\,x > \pi \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}
question_html:
Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point:

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} Kx + 1\,\,if\,x \leq \pi \, \hfill \\ \cos x\,\,if\,\,x > \pi \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}
solution_text: Left hand limit= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {Kx + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {K\left( {\pi - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}= K\pi + 1{/tex} Right hand limit = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \cos x{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \cos \left( {\pi + h} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - \cos \,h{/tex} = -cos 0 = - 1 Therefore, {tex}K\pi + 1 = - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K = \frac{{ - 2}}{\pi }{/tex}
solution_html:
Left hand limit= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {Kx + 1} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {K\left( {\pi - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= K\pi + 1{/tex}
Right hand limit = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \cos x{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \cos \left( {\pi + h} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - \cos \,h{/tex}
= -cos 0 = - 1
Therefore,
{tex}K\pi + 1 = - 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow K = \frac{{ - 2}}{\pi }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} kx + 1,\,\,if\,x \leq 5 \hfill \\ 3x - 5,\,\,if\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 5.
question_html: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} kx + 1,\,\,if\,x \leq 5 \hfill \\ 3x - 5,\,\,if\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at x = 5.
solution_text: Given that, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} kx + 1,\,\,if\,x \leq 5 \hfill \\ 3x - 5,\,\,if\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} When x < 5 we have f(x) = kx + 1 which being a polynomial is continuous at each point x < 5. And, when x > 5, we have f(x) = 3x - 5 which being a polynomial is continuous at each point x > 5. Now {tex}f\left( 5 \right) = 5k + 1 {/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f\left( {5 + h} \right) = 3(5+h)-5= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} (3h+10)=10{/tex} Since function is continuous at x = 5, therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }}{/tex} f(x) = f(5) {tex}\Rightarrow 10 = 5k + 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 5k = 9{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k = \frac{9}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} kx + 1,\,\,if\,x \leq 5 \hfill \\ 3x - 5,\,\,if\,x > 5 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
When x < 5 we have f(x) = kx + 1 which being a polynomial is continuous at each point x < 5. 
And, when x > 5, we have f(x) = 3x - 5 which being a polynomial is continuous at each point x > 5. 
Now  {tex}f\left( 5 \right) = 5k + 1 {/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} f\left( {5 + h} \right) = 3(5+h)-5= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to {0 }} (3h+10)=10{/tex}
Since function is continuous at x = 5, therefore,
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {5^ + }}{/tex}f(x) = f(5)
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 = 5k + 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5k = 9{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k = \frac{9}{5}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the values of a and b such that the function defined by f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}5, & \text { if } x \leq 2 \\ a x+b, & \text { if } 2<x<10 \\ 21, & \text { if } x \geq 10\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.
question_html:
Find the values of a and b such that the function defined by
f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}5, & \text { if } x \leq 2 \\ a x+b, & \text { if } 2<x<10 \\ 21, & \text { if } x \geq 10\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.
solution_text: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 5 , } & { x \leq 2 } \\ { a x + b , } & { 2 < x < 10 } \\ { 21 , } & { x \geq 10 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function. So, it is continuous at x = 2 and at x = 10. {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL x = 2 = RHL x = 2 = f(2) .....(i) and LHL x = 10 = RHL x = 10 = f(10) .....(ii) Now, let us calculate LHL and RHL at x = 2. LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } 5 = 5{/tex} and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( a x + b ){/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \{ a ( 2 + h ) + b \} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 a + a h + b ){/tex} = 2a + b {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 5.........(iii) Now, we have to find LHL and RHL at x = 10. LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } ( a x + b ){/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 10 - h ) + b ]{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 10 a - a h + b ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 10a + b and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } 21{/tex} = 21 Now, from Eq. (ii), we have LHL= RHL {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}10 a + b = 21{/tex} ......(iv) Subtracting Equation (iv) from Equation (iii), {tex}\Rightarrow - 8a = -16{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a = 2{/tex} Putting a = 2 in Equation (iv), 10 (2) + b = 21 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}b = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = 2 \ and\ b = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 5 , } & { x \leq 2 } \\ { a x + b , } & { 2 < x < 10 } \\ { 21 , } & { x \geq 10 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function. So, it is continuous at x = 2 and at x = 10.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}LHLx = 2 = RHLx = 2 = f(2)    .....(i)
and LHLx = 10 = RHLx = 10 = f(10)   .....(ii)
Now, let us calculate LHL and RHL at x = 2.
LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } 5 = 5{/tex}
and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( a x + b ){/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \{ a ( 2 + h ) + b \} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 a + a h + b ){/tex}
= 2a + b
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 5.........(iii)
Now, we have to find LHL and RHL at x = 10.
LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } ( a x + b ){/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 10 - h ) + b ]{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 10 a - a h + b ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}LHL = 10a + b
and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } 21{/tex} = 21
Now, from Eq. (ii), we have
LHL= RHL
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}10 a + b = 21{/tex}   ......(iv)
Subtracting Equation (iv) from Equation (iii), 
{tex}\Rightarrow - 8a = -16{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a = 2{/tex}
Putting a = 2 in Equation (iv), 
10 (2) + b = 21
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}b = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = 2 \ and\ b = 1{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \cos \left( {{x^2}} \right){/tex} is a continuous function.
question_html: Show that the function defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \cos \left( {{x^2}} \right){/tex} is a continuous function.
solution_text: Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2}{/tex} and {tex}g\left( x \right) = \cos x{/tex} , then {tex}\left( {gof} \right)\left( x \right) = g\left[ {f\left( x \right)} \right] = g\left( {{x^2}} \right) = \cos {x^2}{/tex} Now f and g being continuous it follows that their composite (gof) is continuous. Hence {tex}\cos {x^2}{/tex} is continuous function.
solution_html:
Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = {x^2}{/tex} and {tex}g\left( x \right) = \cos x{/tex}, then

{tex}\left( {gof} \right)\left( x \right) = g\left[ {f\left( x \right)} \right] = g\left( {{x^2}} \right) = \cos {x^2}{/tex}

Now f and g being continuous it follows that their composite (gof) is continuous.

Hence {tex}\cos {x^2}{/tex} is continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Show that the function defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right|{/tex} is a continuous function.
question_html: Show that the function defined by {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right|{/tex} is a continuous function.
solution_text: Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right|{/tex} ….(i) f(x) has a real and finite value for all {tex}x \in R.{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f(x) is R. Let g(x) = cos x and {tex}h\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|{/tex} Since g(x) and h(x) being cosine function and modulus function are continuous for all real x Now, {tex}\left( {goh} \right)x = g\left\{ {h\left( x \right)} \right\} = g\left( {\left| x \right|} \right) = \cos \left| x \right|{/tex} being the composite function of two continuous functions is continuous, but not equal to f(x) Again, {tex}\left( {hog} \right)x = h\left\{ {g\left( x \right)} \right\} = h\left( {\cos x} \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right| = f\left( x \right){/tex} [Using eq. (i)] Therefore, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right| = \left( {hog} \right)x{/tex} being the composite function of two continuous functions is continuous.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right|{/tex} ….(i)

f(x) has a real and finite value for all {tex}x \in R.{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f(x) is R.

Let g(x) = cos x and {tex}h\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|{/tex} 

Since g(x) and h(x) being cosine function and modulus function are continuous for all real x 

Now, {tex}\left( {goh} \right)x = g\left\{ {h\left( x \right)} \right\} = g\left( {\left| x \right|} \right) = \cos \left| x \right|{/tex} being the composite function of two continuous functions is continuous, but not equal to f(x) 

Again, {tex}\left( {hog} \right)x = h\left\{ {g\left( x \right)} \right\} = h\left( {\cos x} \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right| = f\left( x \right){/tex}[Using eq. (i)]

Therefore, {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| {\cos x} \right| = \left( {hog} \right)x{/tex} being the composite function of two continuous functions is continuous.

question_text: Examine that {tex}\sin \left| x \right|{/tex} is a continuous function.
question_html: Examine that {tex}\sin \left| x \right|{/tex} is a continuous function.
solution_text: Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|{/tex} and {tex}g\left( x \right) = \sin \left| x \right|{/tex} , then {tex}\left( {gof} \right)x = g\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\} = g\left( {\left| x \right|} \right) = \sin \left| x \right|{/tex} Now, f and g being continuous, it follows that their composite, (gof) is continuous. Therefore, {tex}\sin \left| x \right|{/tex} is continuous.
solution_html:
Let {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|{/tex} and {tex}g\left( x \right) = \sin \left| x \right|{/tex}, then

{tex}\left( {gof} \right)x = g\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\} = g\left( {\left| x \right|} \right) = \sin \left| x \right|{/tex}

Now, f and g being continuous, it follows that their composite, (gof) is continuous.

Therefore, {tex}\sin \left| x \right|{/tex} is continuous.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find all the points of discontinuity of f defined by f(x) = |x| – |x + 1|.
question_html: Find all the points of discontinuity of f defined by f(x) = |x| – |x + 1|.
solution_text: It is given that f(x) = |x| - |x + 1| The given function f is defined for real number and f can be written as the composition of two functions, as f = goh, where g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| Then, f = g - h First we have to prove that g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| are continuous functions. g(x) = |x| can be written as {tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now, g is defined for all real number. Let k be a real number. Case I: If k < 0, Then g(k) = -k And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} ( - x) = - k{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = g(k){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > 0| Case II: If k > 0, Then g(k) = k and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} x = k{/tex} Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{g}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{g}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < 0. Case III: If k = 0, Then, g(k) = g(0) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} ( - x) = 0{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (x) = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = g(0){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at x = 0 From the above 3 cases, we get that g is continuous at all points. g(x) = |x + 1| can be written as {tex}g(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - (x + 1),{\text{ if }}x < - 1} \\ {x + 1,{\text{ if }}x \geqslant - 1} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} Now, h is defined for all real number. Let k be a real number. Case I: If k < -1, Then h(k) = -(k + 1) And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} [ - ({\text{x}} + 1)] = - ({\text{k}} + 1){/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{h}}({\text{k}}){/tex} Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < -1 Case II : If k > -1, Then h(k) = k + 1 and {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 1) = k + 1{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = h(k){/tex} Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > -1. Case III: If k = -1, Then, h(k) = h(-1) = -1 + 1 = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex} [-(x + 1)] = -(-1 + 1) = 0 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (x + 1){/tex} = -(-1 + 1) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = h( - 1){/tex} Therefore, g is continuous at x = -1 From the above 3 cases, we get that h is continuous at all points. Hence, g and h are continuous function. Therefore, f = g – h is also a continuous function.
solution_html:
It is given that f(x) = |x| - |x + 1|
The given function f is defined for real number and f can be written as the composition of two functions, as
f = goh, where g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1|
Then, f = g - h
First we have to prove that g(x) = |x| and h(x) = |x + 1| are continuous functions.
g(x) = |x| can be written as
{tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {-x, \text { if } x<0} \\ {x, \text { if } x \geq 0} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Now, g is defined for all real number.
Let k be a real number.
Case I: If k < 0,
Then g(k) = -k
And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} ( - x) = - k{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = g(k){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > 0|
Case II: If k > 0,
Then g(k) = k and
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} x = k{/tex}
Thus {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{g}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{g}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < 0.
Case III: If k = 0,
Then, g(k) = g(0) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} ( - x) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (x) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} g(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} g(x) = g(0){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at x = 0
From the above 3 cases, we get that g is continuous at all points.
g(x) = |x + 1| can be written as
{tex}g(x) = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - (x + 1),{\text{ if }}x < - 1} \\ {x + 1,{\text{ if }}x \geqslant - 1} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
Now, h is defined for all real number.
Let k be a real number.
Case I: If k < -1,
Then h(k) = -(k + 1)
And {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\text{x}} \to {\text{k}}} [ - ({\text{x}} + 1)] = - ({\text{k}} + 1){/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\mathbf{x}} \to {\mathbf{k}}} {\text{h}}({\text{x}}) = {\text{h}}({\text{k}}){/tex}
Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x < -1
Case II: If k > -1,
Then h(k) = k + 1 and
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to k} (x + 1) = k + 1{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\mathbf{k}}} h(x) = h(k){/tex}
Therefore, h is continuous at all points x, i.e., x > -1.
Case III: If k = -1,
Then, h(k) = h(-1) = -1 + 1 = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} {/tex}[-(x + 1)] = -(-1 + 1) = 0
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} (x + 1){/tex}= -(-1 + 1) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} h(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} h(x) = h( - 1){/tex}
Therefore, g is continuous at x = -1
From the above 3 cases, we get that h is continuous at all points.
Hence, g and h are continuous function.
Therefore, f = g – h is also a continuous function.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\sin \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex}
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\sin \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y = \sin \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \cos \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex} {tex} = \cos \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right)\left( {2x + 0} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2x\cos \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = \sin \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex} 

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \cos \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \cos \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right)\left( {2x + 0} \right){/tex}

{tex}= 2x\cos \left( {{x^2} + 5} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\cos \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} .
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\cos \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex}.
solution_text: Let {tex}y = \cos \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {\sin x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\sin x{/tex} {tex}= - \sin \left( {\sin x} \right)\cos x{/tex} {tex}= - \cos x.\sin \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = \cos \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} 

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \sin \left( {\sin x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\sin x{/tex}

{tex}= - \sin \left( {\sin x} \right)\cos x{/tex}

{tex}= - \cos x.\sin \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\sin \left( {ax + b} \right){/tex}
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\sin \left( {ax + b} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y = \sin \left( {ax + b} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {ax + b} \right){/tex} {tex}= \cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\left( {a + 0} \right){/tex} {tex} = a\cos \left( {ax + b} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = \sin \left( {ax + b} \right){/tex}

{tex}= \cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\left( {a + 0} \right){/tex}

{tex} = a\cos \left( {ax + b} \right){/tex}

question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y = \sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right)\tan \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){/tex} {tex}= \sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right)\tan \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){\sec ^2}\sqrt x \frac{d}{{dx}}\sqrt x {/tex} {tex}= \sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right)\tan \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){\sec ^2}\sqrt {x.} \frac{1}{2}{x^{\frac{1}{2} - 1}}{/tex} {tex}= \sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right)\tan \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right){\sec ^2}\sqrt x .\frac{1}{{2\sqrt x }}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\sec \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right).\tan \left( {\tan \sqrt x } \right).{{\sec }^2}\sqrt x }}{{2\sqrt x }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\frac{{\sin \left( {ax + b} \right)}}{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex}
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x : {tex}\frac{{\sin \left( {ax + b} \right)}}{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}y = \frac{{\sin \left( {ax + b} \right)}}{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex} Using quotient rule, {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\sin \left( {ax + b} \right) - \sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {ax + b} \right) - \sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\left\{ { - \sin \left( {cx + d} \right)} \right\}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\left( a \right) + \sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\sin \left( {cx + d} \right)\left( c \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{a.\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\cos \left( {ax + b} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}} + \frac{{c.\sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\sin \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= a.\cos \left( {ax + b} \right).\sec \left( {cx + d} \right) + c.\sin \left( {ax + b} \right).\tan \left( {cx + d} \right).\sec \left( {cx + d} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y = \frac{{\sin \left( {ax + b} \right)}}{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex} 

Using quotient rule,

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\sin \left( {ax + b} \right) - \sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {ax + b} \right) - \sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\left\{ { - \sin \left( {cx + d} \right)} \right\}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\cos \left( {ax + b} \right)\left( a \right) + \sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\sin \left( {cx + d} \right)\left( c \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{a.\cos \left( {cx + d} \right)\cos \left( {ax + b} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}} + \frac{{c.\sin \left( {ax + b} \right)\sin \left( {cx + d} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {cx + d} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= a.\cos \left( {ax + b} \right).\sec \left( {cx + d} \right) + c.\sin \left( {ax + b} \right).\tan \left( {cx + d} \right).\sec \left( {cx + d} \right){/tex}
